1: The Song of the Sea (Šîrat hayyām) is widely recognized as one of the oldest pieces of poetry in the Hebrew Bible. Linguistic analysis identifies archaic features: the pronominal suffix -môw instead of the later -hemmāh, archaic verbal forms, and metrical patterns that differ from later Hebrew poetry. Many scholars date its composition to the eleventh or twelfth century BCE or even earlier. It follows the literary form of the ANE victory hymn, celebrating YHWH as the triumphant divine warrior in the style of Canaanite battle poetry (especially the Baal cycle of Ugarit), but substituting YHWH for Baal as the cosmic victor.
2: 'The LORD is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation' — this verse was recognized in ancient Israel as a foundational hymn fragment and was quoted verbatim in two later texts: Psalms 118:14 (the great Hallel psalm sung at Passover) and Isaiah 12:2 (the song of the redeemed after YHWH's eschatological salvation). The deliberate re-use of the Song of the Sea's opening in these contexts shows how Israel understood later salvations as replaying the exodus pattern. The phrase zimmirāt YHWH (my strength and song/power) is a single construct noun: YHWH is both the source of strength and the subject of the song.
3: 'The LORD is a warrior (ʾîš milḥāmāh); the LORD is his name' — this is the divine-warrior declaration at the heart of the song. ANE literature abounds with divine-warrior epithets: Baal as the storm-warrior who defeats Yam (Sea) and Mot (Death), Marduk as the warrior who slays Tiamat in Enuma Elish. Israel appropriates this widespread ANE mythological register but redirects it: the warrior who defeats the cosmic sea is YHWH, not the Baal cycle's protagonist. This demythologizes the combat while retaining its power — the victory at the Sea of Reeds is real history, not cosmic myth, but it carries the same theological weight.
4: 'Pharaoh's chariots and his army he cast into the sea; his finest officers are drowned in the Sea of Reeds' — the verb yārāh (cast/throw) may be a wordplay on the same root that underlies tôrāh (teaching/instruction, from the causative 'to point/direct/throw'). The poem celebrates YHWH's 'throwing' Pharaoh's military into the sea just as a warrior throws a weapon. The 'finest officers' (mibḥar šālîšāyw) are the elite chariot officers — the cream of the Egyptian military establishment. Their drowning is a complete reversal of military hierarchy: the most powerful soldiers on earth meet total defeat.
5: 'The great deep (tehōmōt) covered them' — tehōm is the deep-water abyss of Genesis 1:2 that YHWH separated at creation ('the darkness over the face of the deep'). The Song of the Sea deliberately invokes creation language: the same primordial waters that YHWH separated to make the world suitable for life are now the instrument of judgment against those who oppose YHWH's redemptive purpose. The enemy 'went down to the depths like a stone' — precise, vivid, final. This de-creation imagery for judgment appears throughout the prophets and in Revelation's image of the sea giving up its dead (Revelation 20:13).
6: 'Your right hand, O LORD, is majestic in power; your right hand, O LORD, shattered the enemy' — the 'right hand' (yĕmînĕkā) is the place of power and honor in ancient Near Eastern culture; the king placed his trusted officials at his right hand, and military power was concentrated in the right arm. The repetition ('your right hand...your right hand') is a feature of archaic Hebrew poetry's parallelism and possibly indicates a responsorial tradition — different groups singing alternate lines. The theological claim is that YHWH's power is not merely greater than Egypt's but categorically different in kind.
7: 'In the greatness of your majesty (gaʾōnĕkā) you overthrew your adversaries' — gaʾôn (majesty, pride, excellence) is used in Hebrew both positively for divine majesty (Psalms 93:1) and negatively for human arrogance (Isaiah 13:11). The same quality is divine virtue when applied to YHWH and mortal sin when applied to humans — the difference being that YHWH's greatness is the source and standard from which human greatness should derive but doesn't. The fire metaphor ('you unleashed your burning wrath') anticipates the fire of Sinai and the prophetic tradition of YHWH's wrath as fire (Nahum 1:6; Jeremiah 4:4).
8: 'By the blast of your nostrils (bĕrûaḥ ʾappekā) the waters piled up' — the same divine breath (rûaḥ, wind/breath/spirit) that created the world (Genesis 1:2), parted the sea (Exodus 14:21), and will be given at Pentecost (Acts 2) is here personified as YHWH's nostril-blast in battle. The image is visceral and anthropomorphic, drawing on ancient combat imagery where the warrior's snorting breath drives his enemies before him. The 'flowing waters stood upright like a wall' is poetic recollection of the prose description in Exodus 14:22 — the poem and prose narratives are two perspectives on the same event.
9: 'The enemy said, “I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide the plunder”' — the enemy's boastful interior monologue is one of the most effective rhetorical devices in ancient Hebrew poetry. The sevenfold 'I will' (five explicit verbs plus two implied) mimics the structure of divine declaration but issues from a mortal who has no power to guarantee his own words. The contrast with what actually happened — complete drowning — is devastating. The enemy's self-confident calculation ('my hand shall destroy them') is the prototype of every human pretension to control outcomes that belong to YHWH alone (cf. Isaiah 10:8-11; Luke 12:17-20).
10: 'But you blew with your wind (nāšaptā bĕrûḥăkā); the sea covered them' — the abrupt reversal from the enemy's boasting in Exodus 15:9 to the divine action in Exodus 15:10 is compressed into a single breath. The 'you' is emphatic in Hebrew, thrown against the 'my' of the enemy's speech. 'They sank like lead in the mighty waters' — the image is of permanent, irrecoverable submersion. Lead (ʿōpāret) sinks without trace; the Egyptian army has disappeared. The language of sinking 'like lead' appears in a slightly different form in Nehemiah 9:11, showing the Song's continued influence on Israel's liturgical memory.
11: 'Who is like you, O LORD, among the gods?' — this is not monotheism in the later philosophical sense but an incomparability declaration, a common genre in ANE praise literature. The gods (ʾēlîm) are not denied existence here; the claim is that none can compare to YHWH in power, holiness, or awesome deeds. This incomparability declaration is the primary form of Israel's early monotheism: not 'there are no other gods' but 'no other god is in YHWH's category.' The same declaration appears in Psalms 89:6-8; Exodus 86:8; Jeremiah 10:6. By the postexilic period, the incomparability declaration had fully absorbed the denial of other gods' reality (Isaiah 44:6-8; Exodus 45:5-7).
12: 'You stretched out your right hand; the earth swallowed them' — a shift in imagery from drowning (water) to swallowing (earth/Sheol). The Hebrew tiblaʿēmô ʾāreṣ (the earth swallowed them) uses the same verb as Korah's rebellion where the ground opened and swallowed the rebels (Numbers 16:32). The movement from sea to earth in the Song may reflect the ANE cosmic geography where the underworld lay beneath both sea and earth — enemies swallowed by the sea go to the realm of death. YHWH's power extends throughout the vertical cosmos: sky (pillar of cloud), sea (walls of water), and earth (swallowing) are all his instruments.
13: 'In your steadfast love (ḥeseḏ) you have led the people you redeemed' — the Song pivots from past act (the sea crossing) to future destination (the land). Ḥeseḏ here is YHWH's covenant loyalty — the quality that makes his redemption reliable and his leadership dependable. The 'holy abode' (nĕwēh qoḏšĕkā) refers to the promised land in its entirety, not yet to Jerusalem or the temple (which are anticipated in Exodus 15:17). The song is sung before Israel has entered Canaan — it celebrates a future that is so certain because YHWH's covenant love guarantees it that it can be sung in the past tense as already accomplished.
14: 'The peoples heard and trembled; anguish seized the inhabitants of Philistia' — the song anticipates the geopolitical effect of the exodus. The 'nations' who tremble include Philistia, Edom, and Moab — precisely the peoples Israel will encounter on the way to and at the edge of Canaan. The fulfillment of this prediction is confirmed by Rahab in Jericho: 'We have heard how the LORD dried up the water of the Red Sea before you when you came out of Egypt... as soon as we heard it, our hearts melted, and there was no spirit left in any man because of you' (Joshua 2:10-11). The Song anticipates what the Joshua narrative confirms.
15: 'Then the chiefs of Edom were terrified; the leaders of Moab were seized with trembling' — Edom and Moab were Israel's closest ethnic relatives (Edom descended from Esau, Moab from Lot) but would become persistent opponents. Their terror at YHWH's acts is documented in Numbers' accounts of Israel's later encounters with them (Num 20-25). 'The inhabitants of Canaan have melted away' — the same language Rahab uses in Joshua 2:9 (nāmōg, melted away). The Song of the Sea was prophetically accurate: its prediction of the nations' terror at YHWH's acts was fulfilled in the Canaanite period.
16: 'Terror and dread fall on them; by the power of your arm they are still as stone, until your people pass by, O LORD' — the language of ʾêmātāh wāfaḥad (terror and dread) is an idiom for the fear that YHWH's presence produces in his enemies, related to the concept of the divine warrior's battle-shout that paralyzes opponents. The image 'still as stone' suggests complete paralysis — the nations are immobilized while YHWH's people pass through their territory. The theology of divine protection-in-transit becomes a theme in the prophetic tradition: YHWH will clear the way for his people's return from exile (Isaiah 40:3-4; Exodus 52:12).
17: 'You will bring them in and plant them (tiṭṭāʿēmô) on your own mountain — the place, O LORD, you made for your own dwelling' — the 'mountain of YHWH's inheritance' is Zion/Jerusalem, not yet conquered or even named. The Song anticipates the entire trajectory of Israelite history from the sea to the temple: exodus → wilderness → conquest → monarchy → temple. The planting metaphor (tiṭṭāʿēmô) appears also in Psalms 80:8-9 (Israel as a vine transplanted from Egypt) and Isaiah 5:1-7 (the vineyard parable). The sanctuary 'your hands established' points forward to the Solomonic temple, which is described as built by YHWH's own design (1 Chronicles 28:19).
18: 'The LORD will reign forever and ever' — the Song concludes with a declaration of YHWH's eternal kingship that is the theological climax of the entire exodus narrative. This kingship declaration forms the backbone of Israel's liturgical tradition: Psalms 93:1; Exodus 96:10; Exodus 97:1; 99:1 all begin 'The LORD reigns' and are likely connected to ancient enthronement festivals. The kingdom of YHWH is the meta-narrative of the OT that the NT announces as having arrived in Jesus: 'The kingdom of God is at hand' (Mark 1:15) and 'The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever' (Revelation 11:15) both draw directly on Exodus 15:18.
19: The prose recapitulation (Exodus 15:19) summarizes the crossing before the scene transitions to Miriam's antiphon. This alternation of poetry and prose at key narrative moments (also at the crossing of the Jordan in Josh 3-5, with Ps 114 as the poetry) is a feature of biblical historiography: the prose provides the narrative; the poetry provides the theological interpretation. The repetition serves a liturgical purpose: the song was composed to be sung, and the prose surroundings kept it embedded in the historical event rather than floating free as purely cultic poetry.
20: Miriam is the first person in the Bible called a nĕbîʾāh (prophetess); later prophetesses include Deborah (Judges 4:4), Huldah (2 Kings 22:14), and Noadiah (Nehemiah 6:14). She is identified as Aaron's sister (and by extension Moses's sister, matching the figure in Exodus 2), establishing a family leadership triad — Moses, Aaron, and Miriam — that recurs in Numbers (Num 12; 20). The women's tambourine-and-dance celebration of military victory was an established Israelite custom: Jephthah's daughter greets him with tambourines (Judges 11:34), and the women sing over David's victory with tambourines and dancing (1 Samuel 18:6-7). This was the traditional role of women in victory celebration.
21: Miriam's response — 'Sing to the LORD, for he has triumphed gloriously; horse and rider he has thrown into the sea' — is identical to Moses's opening verse (15:1b). This antiphonal structure (leader sings, congregation responds) reflects ancient Israelite worship: the people repeat or respond to the cantor's lead. Some scholars argue that Miriam's verse is the original Song of the Sea, with Moses's longer version (Exodus 15:1-18) being the literary expansion. Either way, the antiphonal structure points to communal, participatory worship rather than solo performance — the entire people, men with Moses and women with Miriam, sing together.
22: The transition from the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15:1-21) to the wilderness journey (15:22ff.) is abrupt and deliberately so: the celebration ends and the testing begins immediately. 'Moses led Israel away from the Sea of Reeds, and they went into the wilderness of Shur' — Shur is the wilderness east of Egypt, stretching from the Nile delta toward the Sinai Peninsula. 'Three days in the wilderness and found no water' establishes the pattern of the wilderness testing: the people are placed under pressure (thirst, hunger, enemies) to reveal what is in their hearts (Deuteronomy 8:2-3). The three-day motif recurs throughout the OT as the testing period before divine provision.
23: Marah (mārāh, 'bitter') gave its name to a place of bitter water — a likely site near modern ʿAyn Hawarah in the Sinai, where the water is brackish. The bitterness of the water becomes a symbol for the bitterness of Israel's situation and its complaint. The Hebrew root mārāh (bitter, to be bitter) is also the root of 'Naomi' (pleasantness) → 'Mara' (bitter) in Ruth 1:20: 'Do not call me Naomi; call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me.' The Marah episode introduces the bitterness motif that will recur throughout the wilderness narrative as Israel's alternating experience of divine provision and complaint.
24: 'The people grumbled (wayyillônû) against Moses' — this is the first use of lûn (to murmur, grumble) in the exodus narrative; the verb will become virtually the signature word of the wilderness generation (Exodus 16:2, 7, 8; Numbers 14:2, 27, 36; Exodus 16:11). The grumbling is always framed as directed against Moses but evaluated by YHWH as directed against himself (Exodus 16:8: 'your grumbling is not against us but against YHWH'). The murmuring motif is what defines the wilderness generation negatively in both the OT summaries (Psalms 106:25; Nehemiah 9:17) and the NT's use of it as a warning (1 Corinthians 10:10; Philippians 2:14).
25: YHWH 'showed him a piece of wood' (ʿēṣ); Moses threw it into the water and it became sweet. The mechanism is not explained — the focus is on divine instruction, human obedience, and divine result. YHWH then 'tested him there' — the same verb (nāsāh) used for testing/proving; the wilderness is explicitly a site of divine testing (Deuteronomy 8:2). The wood-sweetening-bitter-waters image generated a rich tradition of interpretation: the rabbis connected it to the Torah as the 'tree of life' (Proverbs 3:18) that sweetens the bitterness of life; Christian interpreters saw a proto-type of the cross transforming judgment into salvation.
26: 'If you will carefully listen to the voice of the LORD your God' — the conditional covenant structure appears for the first time in the narrative: obedience leads to health and blessing; disobedience leads to the diseases of Egypt. 'I am YHWH your healer (YHWH rophekā)' is one of the compound divine names revealing aspects of YHWH's character — here: YHWH as physician. The other compound names appear throughout Exodus and the wilderness narratives: YHWH-Nissi (Exodus 17:15), YHWH-Mĕqaddĕškem (Exodus 31:13). The promise 'none of the diseases of Egypt' ties obedience to the liberation narrative: Egypt's diseases are what YHWH used against Pharaoh; covenant obedience puts Israel on the other side of that judgment.
27: Elim — 'twelve springs and seventy palm trees' — provides abundant, sweet water after Marah's bitter water. The numbers are likely symbolic as well as literal: twelve corresponds to the twelve tribes of Israel; seventy corresponds to the seventy elders (Exodus 24:1; Numbers 11:16) and the seventy nations of Genesis 10. Elim is the provision that follows testing — the pattern of wilderness theology in miniature. It foreshadows the abundance YHWH intends for his people but which requires the testing period to develop the trust and obedience that can receive it rightly. Elim is probably identified with modern Wadi Gharandel, a well-watered oasis on the western Sinai coast.
